title-group.article-title: Experimental Platform for Screening and Validation of BacNav Gene Therapy Candidates

contrib-group.aff: Department of Biomedical Engineering, Duke University, Durham, NC

author-notes.corresp: Correspondence: Nenad Bursac, PhD, Professor of Biomedical Engineering, Professor of Cell Biology, Associate Professor of Medicine, Duke University, 101 Science Drive, FCIEMAS, room 1427, Durham, NC 27708, Fax: 919-684-4488, nbursac@duke.edu

license.license-p: This file is available for text mining. It may also be used consistent with the principles of fair use under the copyright law.

body.p: Impaired cardiac conduction increases vulnerability to reentrant arrhythmias and sudden cardiac death. We previously showed that NavSheP D60A, an engineered prokaryotic voltage-gated sodium channel (BacNav), can augment electrical excitability and reduce arrhythmia incidence in cardiomyocyte (CM) cultures and infarcted primate hearts1, 2. Unlike large mammalian Nav genes, BacNav genes are small (<1kb) and readily packageable in adeno-associated viral (AAV) vectors. While the NavSheP channel is a promising gene therapy candidate, it has slower gating kinetics than cardiac Nav1.5 channels2. BacNav orthologs with faster kinetics could more effectively improve peak INa and cardiac conduction slowing. More generally, engineered BacNavs with different biophysical properties could be customized for different therapeutic applications3.

To identify novel BacNav orthologs with fast gating kinetics (Figure A), we first searched the UniProt bacterial database for sequences homologous to NavSheP4, with the requisite presence of (1) C-terminal coiled-coil region, (2) selectivity filter (e.g. TLESW), and (3) conserved arginine residues in the S4 region. The top 50 identified BacNav orthologs were human-codon optimized, synthesized, and subcloned to generate CMV-BacNav-GFP lentiviruses, each of which was used to transduce 293KC cells (a monoclonal HEK293 line stably expressing Kir2.1 and connexin-43)2. Nav1.5-GFP- and NavSheP-GFP-expressing 293KC cells served as control. Whole-cell voltage-clamp recordings of GFP+ 293KC cells revealed measurable Na+ currents for 29/50 BacNav orthologs (Figure Bi). Recorded currents exhibited a wide range of gating kinetics (lowest activation (τm) and inactivation (τh) time constants within the recorded membrane voltage (Vm) range, Figure Bii, top) and Vm dependencies (V1/2, half-activation Vm, Figure Bii, bottom).

Notably, the V1/2 for the majority of the 29 channels was considerably different from Nav1.5 (−35 mV) (Figure Bii, bottom), with only 7 of the 29 yielding successful action potential (AP) conduction in 293KC monolayers (data not shown). We next selected the 2 BacNav orthologs with fastest conduction velocity (CV), namely NavRhi from Rhizobiales bacterium and NavXan from Xanthomonadaceae bacterium, to generate monoclonal 293KC lines for comparisons with a monoclonal NavSheP+Kir2.1+Cx43 line. Additionally, we generated a monoclonal 293KC line expressing a D60A mutant of NavMarP from Marinomonas Polaris (to right-shift its hyperpolarized Vm-dependence)4. Optical mapping revealed that both NavXan and NavRhi had faster CVs than NavSheP, but only NavRhi exhibited shorter AP duration (APD) and significantly increased maximum capture rate (MCR), consistent with faster kinetics (Figure Ci). Additionally, the D60A mutation enabled AP conduction in NavMarP 293KC monolayers, demonstrating the ability to tailor BacNav function through site-directed mutagenesis.

Overall, from all 50 candidates, NavRhi exhibited the most favorable electrophysiological properties, with comparable late sodium current to that of Nav1.5 (Figure Cii). Significantly higher CV and MCR of NavRhi than NavSheP prompted us to perform further validations in CMs. Previously, we showed that NavSheP expression significantly improved CV in relatively slow-conducting human iPSC-derived and fibrotic neonatal rat CM monolayers2. NavRhi similarly improved CV in hiPSC-CM monolayers (Figure Di) without changing AP shape or duration (Figure Dii). Considering its significantly faster kinetics, we further tested if NavRhi could also improve conduction in a setting of higher CM excitability using engineered 3D neonatal rat cardiac tissues (“cardiobundles”, Figure E). Unlike NavSheP, NavRhi significantly improved cardiobundle CV by 15.4%, to 51.8±1.9 cm/s, without altering APD.

Encouraged by these results, we assessed NavRhi effects in Scn5a+/− mice, (Figure Fi), in which global heterozygous knockout of Scn5a leads to a ~50% reduction in Nav1.5 gene expression (Figure Fii). The 13.5±1-month old mice received retro-orbital injection of myoAAV2A-MHCK7-NavRhi-HA-mScarlet or control myoAAV2A-MHCK7-mScarlet virus5 (1×1013 vg/kg). Wild-type (WT) littermates received vehicle solution. Ten weeks post-injection, immunostaining confirmed robust ventricular transgene expression (Figure Fiii). Super-resolution imaging demonstrated NavRhi localization in CM t-tubules (Figure Fiv), similar to previously observed NavSheP distribution in mouse and primate hearts2. Quantitative qPCR revealed ~1.6-fold higher NavRhi vs. Scn5a gene copy number in transduced Scn5a+/− ventricles (0.76-fold of the Scn5a+/+ expression, Figure Fv). Considering ~60% transduction efficiency (Figure Fiii) and homotetrameric nature of BacNav channels, the effective NavRhi gene expression per CM was ~66% of the Scn5a expression in WT mice. Importantly, optical mapping of the right ventricle revealed a large conduction slowing in Scn5a+/− mice injected with the control virus, and significant improvement by NavRhi expression to levels approaching WT littermates (Figure Fvi).

Here, we for the first time exploited rich bacterial diversity to screen, identify, and validate novel BacNav gene therapy candidates. Specifically, we identified a BacNav ortholog NavRhi that effectively improved cardiac AP conduction in engineered cardiac tissues in vitro and murine Scn5a+/− model of reduced excitability ex vivo. Customizing biophysical properties of BacNav channels could enable new precision gain-of-excitability therapies targeting deficiencies in cardiac conduction (e.g. Brugada syndrome, heart failure) and other excitable tissue disorders (e.g. Dravet syndrome, hypokalemic periodic paralysis).

This study was conducted in accordance with protocols approved by the Duke University Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC). The use of iPSC and HEK cell lines were exempt from IRB approval. All data is available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

ack.title: Acknowledgments:
ack.p: We thank Dr. Dan Roden for the creation and Dr. Barry London for providing the Scn5a+/− mouse model.

sec.title: Sources of Funding:
sec.p: American Heart Association Predoctoral Fellowship 826228 to T.W., NIH grants EB032726, HL126524, HL132389, HL134764, HL160654 to N.B.

caption.title: Discovery and validation of novel BacNav gene therapy candidates for cardiac conduction slowing.
caption.p: A, Experimental method to identify and characterize novel BacNav orthologs. Bi, Representative INa traces in response to voltage steps from a holding transmembrane potential (Vm) of −80mV (NavSheP, NavXan, NavRhi, NavMarE), −120mV (NavMarP), or −140mV (NavThiS) to testing Vm from −20mV to 30mV. Bii, Time constants of activation (τm) and inactivation (τh), and Vm of half-activation (V1/2) obtained by voltage-clamping of 293KC cells (n=3–5) expressing 29 novel BacNav orthologs shown by respective numbers. V1/2 was derived from Boltzmann equation fitting of the steady-state activation. Nav1.5 and NavSheP are shown for reference. Orthologs labeled with “*” enabled action potential conduction when expressed in 293KC cells. Patch clamp in B was performed at 25°C. Ci, Results of optical mapping in monoclonal 293KC monolayers transduced with NavSheP and 3 novel BacNav. A D60A mutation in NavMarP enabled AP firing (n=8). Cii, Late current, INa,L, quantified under the activation protocol from Bi by averaging INa between 150ms and 250ms of the 500ms depolarization pulse at different test-potentials (Vm), shown as a percentage of the peak INa at that Vm (n=5–7). Di-ii, Representative isochrone maps with corresponding quantification of CV (Di) and optically recorded AP traces with corresponding APD (Dii) in hiPSC-CM monolayers paced at 1 Hz (n=7–9). Pulse signs in Di indicate locations of pacing electrode. E, Representative isochrone maps and corresponding quantification of CV and APD in optically mapped NRVM cardiobundles paced at 2 Hz (n=10–18). Pulse signs indicate locations of pacing electrode. Fi, Experimental timeline of AAV-NavRhi studies in a murine Scn5a+/− model. Fii, mRNA levels of connexin-43 (Gja1) and Nav1.5 (Scn5a) in Scn5a+/− mice hearts, shown normalized to expression in WT (Scn5a+/+) mice (n=6). Fiii, Representative images of immunostained BacNav-HA (left) and mScarlet (right) in mouse ventricles 10 weeks after AAV treatment and corresponding quantification of transduction efficiency. Fiv, Super-resolution image showing sarcolemmal t-tubular NavRhi expression in murine ventricular CMs. Fv, Quantification of gene copy (gc) number of Scn5a and NavRhi, shown normalized to Scn5a expression in WT mice (n=4). Fvi, Representative isochrone maps and corresponding quantification of CV in mouse right ventricles paced at 4Hz. Pulse signs indicate locations of pacing electrode. Data: mean values ± s.e.m. Significance was evaluated by ANOVA tests using GraphPad Prism. Shapiro-Wilk test was used to confirm normality. All rodents were treated ethically in accordance with protocols approved by the Duke University Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC).
